title: Sudden severe upper abdominal pain

chart.oneLiner: 58M presents with the abrupt onset 4 hours ago of severe, constant epigastric pain that rapidly spread across the whole abdomen, now lying rigidly still.

opening: It hit me out of nowhere a few hours ago — a stabbing pain in the pit of my stomach, and now it hurts everywhere and I can't move without it getting worse.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old man, lying very still on the stretcher with knees drawn up, breathing shallowly (deep breaths and any movement worsen the pain), guarding his abdomen. Anxious but cooperative.

HPI: Pain began ABRUPTLY about 4 hours ago — he can name the exact moment — as a sudden, severe, stabbing pain in the epigastrium. Within an hour it spread to involve the WHOLE abdomen and is now constant and “knife-like.” Movement, coughing, and the ambulance ride all made it dramatically worse; lying perfectly still helps a little. Nausea, no significant vomiting. He has had weeks of intermittent gnawing epigastric “burning” before meals relieved by antacids (only if asked about prior stomach symptoms). Takes ibuprofen most days for chronic back pain and bought more over the counter recently (only if asked about medications/painkillers). NO black or bloody stools that he has noticed, NO prior abdominal surgery, NO chest pain or exertional component, NO recent fatty-meal trigger, NO jaundice, NO change in bowels today, NO known gallstones.
PMH: chronic low back pain; mild hypertension. MEDS: over-the-counter ibuprofen most days (if asked); occasional antacids. SOCIAL: smokes ~1 pack/day for 30 years; drinks 3–4 beers most evenings (sensitive — only if asked directly); no recreational drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: diaphoretic, lying rigidly still, in obvious distress.
- Vitals: BP 104/68, HR 118, RR 24, 38.1°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Abdomen: rigid, board-like abdomen with diffuse involuntary guarding and marked rebound tenderness in all quadrants; maximal tenderness in the epigastrium. Bowel sounds ABSENT/markedly reduced. No distension yet; no surgical scars.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs: clear, shallow respirations.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the NSAID/ibuprofen use or the weeks of prior dyspepsia unless asked about medications, painkillers, or prior stomach symptoms.
- Do NOT volunteer the alcohol amount unless asked directly about drinking.
- The peritoneal signs (rigidity, guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds) are revealed only if the abdomen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 16.8 with neutrophil predominance and left shift, Hgb 14.6, Plt 280 (leukocytosis consistent with peritonitis).
labs.BMP: Mild prerenal azotemia (BUN/Cr mildly up), HCO3 19 with anion gap — early lactic/metabolic derangement from peritonitis/hypoperfusion.
labs.Lipase: Mildly elevated but NOT in the thousands — argues against primary acute pancreatitis as the cause.
labs.LFTs: Within normal limits; no obstructive pattern (argues against biliary obstruction/cholangitis).
labs.Lactate: Elevated at 3.2 mmol/L — marker of hypoperfusion/peritonitis, supports urgent source control.
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: free intraperitoneal air and a small amount of free fluid, with focal wall discontinuity of the duodenal bulb/gastric antrum — consistent with a perforated peptic ulcer. No bowel-wall pneumatosis or mesenteric occlusion.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~118 bpm; no ST-elevation, no ischemic ST/T changes — a reactive tachycardia, not an acute coronary event.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia at ~118 bpm with no acute ischemic changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Free air under the right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on the upright film; lungs clear, no consolidation — the classic radiographic sign of a perforated viscus.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on an upright chest film with clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum)
searchTerms: free air under diaphragm chest x-ray

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Perforated peptic ulcer / perforated viscus (leading,8). Mesenteric ischemia (CANT-MISS to exclude in a vasculopath with peritonitis and lactate,5). Acute pancreatitis (core,3). Ruptured/symptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm (core,2). Acute cholecystitis/biliary disease (core,2). Names a surgical abdomen / peritonitis and the imperative to obtain urgent imaging for free air and a surgical opinion (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Perforated peptic ulcer (perforated viscus). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Mesenteric ischemia. Also reasonable: Acute pancreatitis; Ruptured/symptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm; Acute cholecystitis.

coreActions.item: Names a surgical abdomen / peritonitis and the imperative to obtain urgent imaging for free air and a surgical opinion

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Upright chest X-ray (or upright/lateral decubitus abdominal film) for free air (CANT-MISS,4). CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (CANT-MISS,5). CBC (2). BMP with lactate (2). Lipase to assess for pancreatitis (1). Type & screen / coags pre-op (bonus,1). PENALTY: ordering an upper GI series with barium in a suspected perforation (barium peritonitis) (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Upright chest X-ray for free air; CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast; CBC; BMP with lactate.

criticalActions.item: Upright chest X-ray (or decubitus abdominal film) for free air

penalties.item: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)

requiredTests: Upright chest X-ray (or decubitus abdominal film) for free air
contraindicatedTests: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)
orderSensitiveActions: Barium upper GI series in suspected perforation (risk of barium peritonitis)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes no acute ischemic changes — a reactive tachycardia (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia / mislabeling as ACS (-3).
keyFindings: no acute ST-segment or T-wave ischemic changes
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes (reactive to pain/peritonitis).
commonMistakes: over-calling subtle ST/T changes as an acute coronary syndrome
attributing the abdominal pain to a cardiac cause
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~118 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic ST/T changes — a reactive tachycardia from pain and peritonitis, not ACS.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes (reactive to pain/peritonitis).
coreActions: no acute ST-segment or T-wave ischemic changes

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's upright chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum). CREDIT: identifies free air under the diaphragm (6); recognizes it as evidence of a perforated viscus needing urgent surgery (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the free air (-3).
keyFindings: free air (lucency) under the right hemidiaphragm
recognizes this as pneumoperitoneum from a perforated viscus
criticalFindings: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
commonMistakes: mistaking the subdiaphragmatic free air for bowel gas (Chilaiditi) or calling the film normal
missing the crescent of air on an underpenetrated film
steps.idealAnswer: Free air under the right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on the upright film with clear lungs — diagnostic of a perforated hollow viscus requiring urgent surgical evaluation.

criticalActions: Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) — perforated viscus.
coreActions: free air (lucency) under the right hemidiaphragm
recognizes this as pneumoperitoneum from a perforated viscus

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Free air under the diaphragm on CXR, free intraperitoneal air with focal duodenal/antral wall discontinuity on CT, leukocytosis, elevated lactate, and only mildly elevated lipase. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Perforated peptic ulcer (perforated viscus) (6). Cites free air plus focal wall defect with leukocytosis; explicitly excludes primary pancreatitis (lipase not markedly elevated) and mesenteric ischemia (no vascular occlusion/pneumatosis on CT) (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH surgical abdomen / GI bleed & PUD: NPO, large-bore IV access, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (CANT-MISS,6); urgent surgical consult for operative repair (omental/Graham patch) (CANT-MISS,6); broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gut flora (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) (4); nasogastric decompression and IV PPI (3); analgesia, antiemetics, type & screen, monitor (3); test/treat H. pylori and stop NSAIDs on recovery (3). PENALTY: discharging or observing a peritonitic patient with free air (-4); delaying surgical consult / source control (-2); barium study (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: NPO, two large-bore IVs, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation, monitor. Urgent surgical consultation for operative repair (Graham omental patch) and peritoneal washout. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric flora (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). Nasogastric tube decompression and IV proton-pump inhibitor. Analgesia, antiemetics, type & screen, foley to monitor output. On recovery: stop NSAIDs and test/treat H. pylori.

criticalActions.item: NPO, IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair

coreActions.item: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gut flora

bonusActions.item: Stop NSAIDs and test/treat H. pylori on recovery

penalties.item: Discharging/observing a peritonitic patient with free air

requiredActions: NPO, IV access, aggressive fluid resuscitation
Urgent surgical consult for operative repair
optionalActions: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gut flora
Stop NSAIDs and test/treat H. pylori on recovery
unsafeActions: Discharging/observing a peritonitic patient with free air

disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

historyTriggers.response: It came on completely out of the blue about four hours ago — I can tell you the exact minute it hit me.
It started as a stabbing pain right in the pit of my stomach, and now it's a constant knife-like pain across my whole belly.
Any movement, coughing, or a deep breath makes it much worse — lying perfectly still is the only thing that helps even a little.
Now that you mention it, for the last few weeks I've had a gnawing, burning feeling in my upper stomach before meals — antacids took the edge off.

triggerConcepts: what do you take for your back
historyTriggers.response: I take ibuprofen most days for my back pain — I picked up a big bottle from the pharmacy recently and have been using it more than usual.
No, I haven't noticed any black or bloody stools and I haven't thrown up any blood.
I usually have three or four beers most evenings.
No chest pain and nothing brought it on with effort — it's all in my belly.
I have chronic low back pain and slightly high blood pressure. I've never had any abdominal surgery.
Yes, about a pack a day for thirty years.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58M presents with the abrupt onset 4 hours ago of severe, constant epigastric pain that rapidly spread across the whole abdomen, now lying rigidly still. The diagnosis is Perforated peptic ulcer, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Perforated peptic ulcer while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Perforated peptic ulcer and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
